The user is asking me to explain the principle of dynamic programming. Let me think about what dynamic programming is and its key concepts.

Dynamic programming is an algorithmic technique used to solve optimization problems. The main ideas behind it are:

1. Breaking down complex problems into smaller, simpler subproblems
2. Solving these subproblems once and storing their solutions (memoization or tabulation)
3. Reusing these stored solutions when the same subproblems occur again
4. This works because many problems have overlapping subproblems - the same smaller problems appear multiple times

The key properties that make dynamic programming applicable are:
- Optimal substructure: The optimal solution to the problem can be constructed from optimal solutions of its subproblems
- Overlapping subproblems: The same subproblems are solved multiple times

By storing solutions in a table (tabulation approach) or cache (memoization approach), we avoid redundant computations, which significantly reduces time complexity compared to naive recursive approaches.

For example, in computing Fibonacci numbers, a naive recursive approach has exponential time complexity because it recalculates the same values many times. With dynamic programming, we store each Fibonacci number once calculated, reducing the complexity to linear time.

def is_anagram(str1, str2): 
    # Get lengths of both strings 
    n1 = len(str1) 
    n2 = len(str2) 
  
    # If the length of both strings are not same,  
    # then they cannot be anagram 
    if n1 != n2: 
        return 0
  
    # Sort both strings 
    str1 = sorted(str1) 
    str2 = sorted(str2) 
  
    # Compare sorted strings 
    for i in range(0, n1): 
        if str1[i] != str2[i]: 
            return 0
  
    return 1

is_anagram(string1, string2) // 1

using namespace std; 


// Class to represent a Stack

{

    bool empty(); 
    void pop(); 
    int top(); 
}; 

void Stack::push(int data) 
{ 
    // inserting an element 
    // on the top of the stack 
    v.push_back(data); 
} 

bool Stack::empty() 
{ 
    // checking if the stack is empty 
    return v.size() == 0; 
} 

void Stack::pop() 
{ 
    // removing element

    if (!empty())

        v.pop_back(); 
    } 
}

{ 
    // returns the top element 
    // of the stack 
    return v.back(); 
}

   - If divisible by any number in range, return false
   - Otherwise return true

class PrimeNumber
{
    public static void Main()
    {
        Console.Write("Enter a number: ");
        int n = int.Parse(Console.ReadLine());
 
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++)
        {
            if (CheckPrime(i))
            {
                Console.Write(i + " ");
            }
        }
        Console.WriteLine();
    }
 
    public static bool CheckPrime(int numberToCheck)
    {
        int boundary = (int)Math.Floor(Math.Sqrt(numberToCheck));
 
        if (numberToCheck == 1) return false;
        if (numberToCheck == 2) return true;
 
        for (int i = 2; i <= boundary; ++i)
        {
            if (numberToCheck % i == 0) return false;
        }
 
        return true;
    }
}

The efficient way to sort the array using Java is to use the Quicksort algorithm which is an in-place, recursive sorting algorithm with an average time complexity of O(n log n). The Quicksort algorithm works by selecting an element as a pivot, partitioning the array around the pivot, and recursively applying the Quicksort algorithm to the two sub-arrays on either side of the pivot. This process continues until all the elements in the array are sorted.

The code for the Quicksort algorithm in Java will look something like this:

public class QuickSort {
    // Method for rearranging the elements
    public static void sort(int[] arr, int low, int high) {
        // Check for base case
        if (low < high) {
            int pi = partition(arr, low, high);
            // Recursively sort elements before
            // partition and after partition
            sort(arr, low, pi-1);
            sort(arr, pi+1, high);
        }
    }

    // Method for partitioning the array on the basis of the pivot
    private static int partition(int[] arr, int low, int high) {
        int pivot = arr[high];
        int i = low - 1;
        for (int j = low; j < high; j++) {
            // If the current element is smaller than or equal to pivot
            if (arr[j] <= pivot) {
                i++;

                // Swap arr[i] and arr[j]
                int temp = arr[i];
                arr[i] = arr[j];
                arr[j] = temp;
            }
        }

        // Swap arr[i + 1] and arr[high] (or pivot)
        int temp2 = arr[i+1];
        arr[i+1] = arr[high];
        arr[high] = temp2;

        return i + 1;
    }
}
